text: All right, welcome to another episode. Today we're going to be talking about preperitoneal anatomy during inguinal hernia surgery.

This is a straightforward case of a four-year-old man presenting with a symptomatic inguinal hernia, no significant medical or surgical history, and an obvious hernia on exam, so no diagnostic testing was obtained.
The surgical team begins the operation with a transabdominal view, revealing the internal anatomy relevant to the inguinal hernia procedure.

The surgeon visually identifies the deep inguinal ring, vas deferens, and spermatic vessels. The lateral and medial umbilical folds are visible alongside the inferior epigastric vessels. The hernia defect is observed lateral to the epigastric vessels and medial to the deep ring.
So it always raises the possibility, is this a lateral direct hernia? And so here we are fast-forwarding through our dissection to get to the money shot.
The surgeon examines the hernia site, grasping the preperitoneal fat and carefully teasing away the pseudosac and transversalis fascia attached to the distal aspect of the fat.

The surgical view reveals a lateral direct inguinal hernia, with visible anatomical landmarks including the deep ring, vas deferens, spermatic vessels, lateral and medial umbilical folds, and the inferior epigastric vessels.
The surgeon pauses the initial reduction of the hernia upon encountering variant anatomy, focusing instead on developing a more appropriate medial dissection.
The surgeon enters the parietal compartment and develops a lateral dissection, pushing tissue towards the lateral side wall in preparation for mesh placement.
The surgeon performs parietalization of the vas deferens and surrounding vessels, carefully separating these structures from adjacent tissues.

The surgeon returns to the preperitoneal fat hernia, with the epigastric vessels visible medially. The preperitoneal fat is nearly fully reduced, revealing the iliopubic tract and the peritoneal edge. The surgeon focuses on identifying Henle's ligament and Hasselbeck's ligament.
The surgeon identifies the margins of a direct hernia, observing a direct defect located lateral to the epigastric vessels.
The surgeon continues the dissection, showing the spermatic vessels and vas deferens entering the canal independently from the hernia, supported by the transversalis sling.
The surgeon ensures an adequate critical view by dissecting around the hernia, which has been reduced, and begins developing an appropriate pocket for further surgical steps.

One of the other things I'd like to highlight is ways that you can know whether or not you've appropriately parietalized the structures. And in a sec here, I'll show you some more anatomy that I think, especially for trainees and those just starting with preperitoneal, inguinal dissections may intimidate them.
The surgeon's left hand grasps the obliterated umbilical artery, a consistent anatomical landmark, while the vas deferens and peritoneal edge are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to carefully dissect around the identified structure, maintaining caution due to the potential for recanalization. The dissection is visibly well-developed at this stage.

But really, the focus of this video wasn't on the entire operation, but rather the focus on the direct hernia and understanding why this was actually a lateral direct. So hopefully that was helpful for you and educational.
